item.title: History of Weiqi
item.description:
Weiqi’s history is a long record of transmission and institutional refinement: a game that began in China, spread across East Asia, and later became a global competitive and educational tradition.

Worked example: one game, three names

The same game is commonly called weiqi in Chinese, baduk in Korean, and go in Japanese-derived English usage [@britannica2026go; @igf2026historyofgo]. Those names mark more than translation. They show the route by which the game moved across linguistic and institutional settings while keeping a recognizable rules core.

item.title: Komi and Handicap
item.description:
Audience: readers who understand basic weiqi structure and want to know how even games and uneven games are balanced.

Learning goal: explain the difference between komi and handicap, and show why weiqi uses both.

Worked example: even game versus teaching game

In an even game, Black moves first and White receives komi, a fixed score bonus that offsets the first-move advantage [@britgo2025default; @britgo2026whatisgo]. In a teaching game between players of unequal strength, the weaker player may instead receive handicap stones before play begins [@britgo2026whatisgo]. These two adjustments solve different problems. Komi balances the initiative inside an otherwise equal game. Handicap balances the starting position between unequal players.

item.title: Living Groups and Eyes
item.description:
Audience: readers who already understand liberties, capture, territory, and ko.

Learning goal: explain why connection and eye shape determine whether a group lives or dies.

Worked example: a corner group with two eyes

Imagine Black has enclosed a small corner group so that two separate empty points remain inside its shape. White surrounds the outside completely. Even so, Black’s group is usually safe. White cannot simply fill both internal points in sequence, because each point functions as an eye, and the group remains alive as long as those internal liberties stay structurally separate [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

item.title: Weiqi Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who want a first structural map of weiqi before studying joseki, life and death, or advanced strategy.

Learning goal: explain how liberties, capture, territory, and ko organize play in weiqi.

Worked example: surrounding one stone

Imagine an empty 9 by 9 board. Black places one stone near the center. White then places stones on each of the four adjacent intersections around it. Once Black’s stone has no remaining liberties, White removes it from the board. That is a capture.
